original_abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of embedding large-scale knowledge graphs into continuous vector spaces. While the recently proposed TransE method is highly efficient and offers state-of-the-art predictive performance, it struggles to accurately represent complex relational mapping properties such as reflexivity, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many relationships. Although more complex models can capture these properties, they often do so at the cost of computational efficiency. To bridge this gap, we introduce TransH, which models each relation as a translation on a specific hyperplane. This approach effectively preserves intricate mapping properties while maintaining a model complexity nearly identical to that of TransE. Furthermore, recognizing that real-world knowledge graphs are often incomplete, we propose a strategy for generating negative examples that leverages the mapping properties of relations to mitigate the issue of false negative labeling during training. We evaluated TransH through extensive experiments on link prediction, triplet classification, and fact extraction using WordNet and Freebase. Our results demonstrate that TransH significantly outperforms TransE in predictive accuracy while retaining the same high level of scalability.
humanized_abstract: Link Prediction based on Multi-Hyperplane Networks In this paper, we investigate the embedding problem of large-scale knowledge graph. As one of the most famous models, TransE achieved the highest prediction accuracy and efficiency. However, the relational mapping properties such as reflexivity, one-to-many, many-to-one and many-to-many are hard to be preserved by complex models due to the strict constraint of model complexity. In this paper, we propose a new model TransH, which models the relations as the translation on a specific hyperplane, thus preserves the complex mapping properties and keeps the same model complexity with TransE. To alleviate the influence of false negative samples generated by incomplete knowledge graph to the training process, we propose a novel method for generating negative samples based on the mapping properties of the relations. Experiment results on link prediction, triplet classification and fact extraction tasks from WordNet and Freebase show that TransH achieves higher prediction accuracy than the state-of-the-art method TransE while keeps the same high efficiency.

Experiment results on link prediction, triplet classification and fact extraction tasks from WordNet and Freebase show that TransH achieves higher prediction accuracy than the state-of-the-art method TransE while keeps the same high efficiency.
